title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: vignette illustrates main use slicer package means toy example. Empirical distributions generated bivariate Gaussian mixtures four isotropic components laid symmetrically around origin. distributions differ one another rotated counterclockwise angle ω∈[0,π/2)\omega \[0, \pi/2). goal analysis retrieve specific angles distributions rotated.

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: Let’s load slicer. generate input distributions, define generate_data(): plots show two empirical distributions, using rotation angle ω=π/16\omega = \pi/16 using one ω=3π/8\omega = 3\pi/8.
code: library(slicer) generate_data <- function(n = 200, a = 5, angle = 0, Sigma = diag(1, 2)) {   mus <- cbind(c(a, 0), c(0, a), c(-a, 0), c(0, -a))   R <- rbind(     c(cos(angle), -sin(angle)),     c(sin(angle), cos(angle))   )   cluster <- sample(1:4, size = n, replace = TRUE)   d <- matrix(0, nrow = n, ncol = 2)   for (i in 1:4) {     d[which(cluster == i), ] <- MASS::mvrnorm(       sum(cluster == i), mu = mus[, i], Sigma = Sigma     )   }   d %*% t(R) } set.seed(2026) # for reproducibility  op <- par(no.readonly = TRUE) par(mfrow = c(1, 2)) generate_data(angle = pi/16) |>    plot(xlab = "first dimension", ylab = "second dimension", asp = 1) generate_data(angle = 3*pi/8) |>    plot(xlab = "first dimension", ylab = "second dimension", asp = 1) par(op)

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: generate 60 matrices (40 training, 20 testing) containing empirical distributions rotation angles sampled uniformly [0,π/4)[0, \pi/4). empirical distribution based 200 data points.
code: sample_size <- 200 N_train <- 40 N_test  <- 20 angle_range <- c(0, pi/4)  angles <- runif(N_train + N_test, angle_range[1], angle_range[2])  distributions <- vector("list", N_train + N_test) for (i in seq_len(N_train + N_test)) {   distributions[[i]] <- generate_data(angle = angles[i]) }

title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text: can use compute_all_distances() compute pairwise distances input distributions. supply optional vector test_idx test set indices, pairwise distance computations test objects (needed obtaining predictions) skipped. compute sliced Wasserstein distances, need first generate 𝛉𝟏,…,𝛉𝐋\boldsymbol{\theta_1}, \dots, \boldsymbol{\theta_L} sampled uniformly random unit sphere; generate_directions() takes care . ’ll use L=25L = 25 set d=2d = 2 since ’re working two dimensions. ’re interested estimated sliced Wasserstein distances, can set keep_projections FALSE. 60×6060 \times 60 matrix sw_distances contains squared (estimated) sliced Wasserstein distances among forty training objects training objects twenty test objects. pairwise distances among test objects set NA: can also compute (normal) Wasserstein distances distributions projected along certain dimensions. instances, compute pairwise Wasserstein distances along first margin along second margin, set projection directions 𝐞𝟏=(1,0)⊤\boldsymbol{e_1} = (1, 0)^{\top} 𝐞𝟐=(0,1)⊤\boldsymbol{e_2} = (0, 1)^{\top}. also set keep_projections = TRUE, cause function output list two matrices squared Wasserstein distances: one margin.
code: thetas <- generate_directions(L = 25, d = 2) sw_distances <- compute_all_distances(distributions, thetas, verbose = FALSE,     keep_projections = FALSE, test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test)) sw_distances # output not shown marginal_distances <- compute_all_distances(distributions, diag(1, 2),      verbose = FALSE, keep_projections = TRUE,      test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test)) str(marginal_distances) #> List of 2 #>  $ : num [1:60, 1:60] 0 1.274 0.931 0.548 0.649 ... #>  $ : num [1:60, 1:60] 0 2.177 0.121 0.445 0.599 ...

what: Gaussian process models with tuned hyperparameters
title: (Sliced) Wasserstein - Gaussian process regression workflow
text:
function fit_gpr() takes single matrix squared pairwise distances uses input Gaussian process regression model (default) Gaussian RBF kernel: kRBF(x,x′)=s2exp⁡(−d2(x,x′)2t2)k_{	extrm{RBF}}(x, x') = s^2\exp\left(- rac{d^2(x, x')}{2t^2}ight), s2>0s^2 > 0 scaling factor, t>0t > 0 length-scale, d(x,x′)d(x, x') distance inputs xx x′x'. model’s kernel’s hyperparameters tuned using training data minimising negative marginal log-likelihood. output includes predictions test data, covariannce predictions (true test outcomes provided), estimated hyperparameters.  Since didn’t compute distances test inputs, predictions variances reported (main diagonal test_variance): also computed distances test inputs, test_variance also included covariances. function fit_gpr() can also used multiple matrices squared distances provided. Now, estimated length-scale (kernel) variance hyperparameters provided Gaussian RBF corresponding squared distance matrix.  can combine marginal sliced Wasserstein distances list three distance matrices, :  Parallel processing can enabled using cores parameter: Instead Gaussian RBF kernel, Matérn kernels smoothness parameter ν=0.5,1.5,2.5
u = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5 can used. can use different kernel distance matrix; total kernel used conical combination kernels. instance, following fit uses Matérn kernel ν=2.5
u = 2.5 sliced Wasserstein distances, Matérn kernel ν=0.5
u = 0.5 Wasserstein distances along first axis, Matérn kernel ν=1.5
u = 1.5 Wasserstein distances alogn second axis. Finally, GPR’s negative log predictive density (NLPD) test data can obtained like : warning message occurs didn’t compute distances among test inputs . result, kernel matrices don’t specify covariance among test inputs, NLPD assumes zero. remove test_idx parameter setting running compute_all_distances() refit models, warning disappear.
code: sw_fit <- fit_gpr(sw_distances,   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    y_test = angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)],   verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -58.8175077. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -58.8175077 to -58.8176617. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(sw_fit) #> List of 8 #>  $ kernels         : chr "rbf" #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.241 0.7091 0.2402 0.5074 0.0604 ... #>  $ test_variance   : num [1:20, 1:20] 0.00134 NA NA NA NA ... #>  $ RMSE            : num 0.038 #>  $ length_scale    : num 1.57 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num 0.0758 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 1.57e-07 #>  $ nll             : num -58.8 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], sw_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) diag(sw_fit$test_variance) #>  [1] 0.0013366282 0.0021746730 0.0009410588 0.0024842495 0.0009932353 #>  [6] 0.0012133334 0.0026980876 0.0017291003 0.0009257171 0.0010941720 #> [11] 0.0014113678 0.0014527097 0.0008645677 0.0009060975 0.0030615151 #> [16] 0.0008919646 0.0017044887 0.0039857607 0.0025413811 0.0023768280 marginal_fit <- fit_gpr(marginal_distances,   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    y_test = angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)],   verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -48.3151392. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -48.3151392 to -61.5134707. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -61.5134707 to -61.5134707. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(marginal_fit) #> List of 8 #>  $ kernels         : chr [1:2] "rbf" "rbf" #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.2395 0.6678 0.2429 0.5619 0.0919 ... #>  $ test_variance   : num [1:20, 1:20] 0.000587 NA NA NA NA ... #>  $ RMSE            : num 0.0522 #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:2] 3.9 6.09 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:2] 0.347 0.146 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 0.000448 #>  $ nll             : num -61.5 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], marginal_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) total_fit <- fit_gpr(list(sw_distances, marginal_distances[[1]], marginal_distances[[2]]),   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    y_test = angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)],   verbose = TRUE) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 10. #> Optimum set at -58.8320454. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -58.8320454 to -61.5129334. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 10. #> Current optimum improved from -61.5129334 to -63.080251. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 10. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 10. str(total_fit) #> List of 8 #>  $ kernels         : chr [1:3] "rbf" "rbf" "rbf" #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.247 0.69 0.247 0.549 0.085 ... #>  $ test_variance   : num [1:20, 1:20] 0.00081 NA NA NA NA ... #>  $ RMSE            : num 0.047 #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:3] 0.421 3.833 5.482 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:3] 0.00118 0.24703 0.12446 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 3.32e-08 #>  $ nll             : num -63.1 plot(angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)], total_fit$test_predictions,      xlab = "true test outcomes", ylab = "predicted test outcomes", asp = 1) total_fit <- fit_gpr(list(sw_distances, marginal_distances[[1]], marginal_distances[[2]]),   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    y_test = angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)],   runs = 50L, cores = 2L) str(total_fit) #> List of 8 #>  $ kernels         : chr [1:3] "rbf" "rbf" "rbf" #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.247 0.69 0.247 0.549 0.085 ... #>  $ test_variance   : num [1:20, 1:20] 0.00081 NA NA NA NA ... #>  $ RMSE            : num 0.047 #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:3] 0.421 3.836 5.49 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:3] 0.00117 0.24754 0.12492 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 1.05e-07 #>  $ nll             : num -63.1 total_fit_matern <- fit_gpr(list(sw_distances, marginal_distances[[1]], marginal_distances[[2]]),   training_idx = seq_len(N_train),   test_idx = N_train + seq_len(N_test),   y_train = angles[seq_len(N_train)],    y_test = angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)],   kernels = c("matern25", "matern05", "matern15"),   runs = 50L, cores = 2L) str(total_fit_matern) #> List of 8 #>  $ kernels         : chr [1:3] "matern25" "matern05" "matern15" #>  $ test_predictions: num [1:20] 0.254 0.664 0.2329 0.5286 0.0812 ... #>  $ test_variance   : num [1:20, 1:20] 0.0105 NA NA NA NA ... #>  $ RMSE            : num 0.0555 #>  $ length_scale    : num [1:3] 0.421 3.825 5.465 #>  $ scaling_factor  : num [1:3] 0.00118 0.2461 0.1237 #>  $ noise_variance  : num 3.6e-08 #>  $ nll             : num -63.1 nlpd_gpr(total_fit_matern, angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)]) #> Warning in nlpd_gpr(total_fit_matern, angles[N_train + seq_len(N_test)]): The fit's test variance contains NAs, likely because the distances among the test inputs weren't provided. #> Replacing NAs by 0. #> [1] -1.13788 #> attr(,"jitter_used") #> [1] 0

title: Authors and Citation

code: @Manual{,   title = {slicer: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression},   author = {Jan Vanhove},   year = {2026},   note = {R package version 0.0.0.9000},   url = {https://github.com/janhove/slicer}, }

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: slicer provides functions computing sliced Wasserstein distances well one-dimensional Wasserstein distances empirical distributions represented matrices. distances can fed Gaussian process regression model, currently accepts Gaussian radial basis function kernel well Matérn kernels smoothness parameters ν=0.5,1.5,2.5\nu = 0.5, 1.5, 2.5.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: can install development version slicer GitHub :
code: # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("janhove/slicer")

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distances for Regression
text: Please refer vignette brief tutorial.

what: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: function computes squared 2-Wasserstein distances projections several empirical directions along specified projection directions. projection directions can optionally transformed means linear map. functions can output squared distances along projection direction, squared distances averaged projection directions.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
code: compute_all_distances(   distributions,   thetas,   A = NULL,   verbose = TRUE,   keep_projections = TRUE,   test_idx = NULL )

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: distributions list matrices representing empirical distributions. thetas matrix, row represents projection direction. Optionally, matrix used transform projection direction. verbose TRUE, show progress. keep_projections TRUE, distance matrix projection direction output. FALSE, distance matrices different projection directions averaged. test_idx Optionally, vector indices. supplied, skip distance computations distribution pairs whose indices occur vector.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
text: keep_projections = TRUE, list squared-distance matrices, one projection direction; otherwise, matrix averaged squared distances.

title: Pairwise Wasserstein Distances Along Projection Directions Between Several Empirical Distributions — compute_all_distances
code: M1 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M2 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M3 <- matrix(rnorm(250), ncol = 5) # Sliced Wasserstein: my_directions <- generate_directions(20, 5) compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), my_directions,   keep_projections = FALSE, verbose = FALSE) #>           [,1]      [,2]      [,3] #> [1,] 0.0000000 0.3726307 0.2292539 #> [2,] 0.3726307 0.0000000 0.2697058 #> [3,] 0.2292539 0.2697058 0.0000000 # Marginal Wasserstein distances: marginal_wass <- compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), diag(1, 5),   keep_projections = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) marginal_wass[[3]] # along third dimension #>            [,1]      [,2]       [,3] #> [1,] 0.00000000 0.4029302 0.09973663 #> [2,] 0.40293015 0.0000000 0.35042965 #> [3,] 0.09973663 0.3504296 0.00000000 # Reweight projection directions A <- diag(c(4, 0.5, 3, 2, 1)) shear_wass <- compute_all_distances(list(M1, M2, M3), diag(1, 5), A = A,   keep_projections = TRUE, verbose = FALSE) shear_wass[[3]] #>           [,1]     [,2]      [,3] #> [1,] 0.0000000 3.626371 0.8976297 #> [2,] 3.6263714 0.000000 3.1538668 #> [3,] 0.8976297 3.153867 0.0000000

what: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: function generates predictions using Gaussian process model one several Gaussian RBF kernels. hyperparameters tuned using training data minimising model's negative marginal log-likelihood.

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
code: fit_gpr(   D2,   training_idx,   test_idx,   y_train,   y_test = NULL,   centre = TRUE,   use_gradient = TRUE,   kernels = "rbf",   runs = 10L,   cores = 1L,   verbose = TRUE )

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: D2 matrix, list matrices, containing squared pairwise distances objects. training_idx vector row (column) numbers corresponding training entries D2. test_idx vector row (column) numbers corresponding test entries D2. y_train vector training outcomes. y_test Optionally, vector test outcomes. centre TRUE, training outcomes centred around mean fitting model. mean added back predictions end. use_gradient TRUE, closed-form expressions RBF's gradient used. Else, optimiser uses finite-differences method. Ignored non-RBF kernels used. kernels vector kernels ("rbf", "matern05", "matern15", "matern25"). runs Number independent attempts find minimum optimising hyperparameters. cores Number cores used parallel processing. verbose TRUE, progress shown console. works cores == 1L.

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
text: list containing predictions test objects well variance, root mean squared error (true test outcomes provided), three tuned hyperparameter values.

title: Gaussian Process Fitting to Test Data with Tuned Hyperparameters — fit_gpr
Generate Random Projection Directions — generate_directions
text: Obtain L random vectors unit sphere d-dimensional Euclidean space.

title: Generate Random Projection Directions — generate_directions
code: generate_directions(L, d)

title: Generate Random Projection Directions — generate_directions
text: L Number projection directions. d Dimensionality ambient space.

title: Generate Random Projection Directions — generate_directions
text: L d matrix, row contains unit vector.

title: Generate Random Projection Directions — generate_directions

what: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: Computes posterior mean predictions Gaussian process regression model given precomputed kernel matrices. implementation follows Algorithm 2.1 Rasmussen Williams (2006), optional centring step automatic jitter escalation handle near-singular kernel matrices.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
code: gpr_predict(   Kxx,   Kxstar,   y_train,   centre = TRUE,   lambda2 = 1e-06,   Kxstarstar = NULL )

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: Kxx Kernel matrix evaluated training inputs. Kxstar Matrix kernel values test training inputs. row corresponds one test input. y_train Vector training outcomes. centre TRUE, training outcomes centred around mean. lambda2 Kernel noise variance. Kxstarstar Optional. square matrix kernel values among test inputs. NULL (default), posterior means computed returned vector. Else posterior mean vector covariance matrix returned.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
text: Kxstarstar NULL (default), vector posterior mean predictions test inputs. Else, list vector mean posterior mean predictions matrix var posterior covariance test inputs.

title: Predict Outcomes Using Gaussian Process Regression — gpr_predict
code: N1 <- 25 N2 <- 10 x_train <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = N1) x_test  <- runif(N2, -pi, pi) distance <- outer(c(x_train, x_test), c(x_train, x_test), "-") |> abs() kernel <- rbf(distance, length_scale = 1, variance = 1) Kxx    <- kernel[1:N1, 1:N1] Kxstar <- kernel[(N1+1):(N1+N2), 1:N1] Kxstarstar <- kernel[(N1+1):(N1+N2), (N1+1):(N1+N2)] y_train <- ifelse(x_train == 0, 2*pi, sin(2*pi*x_train) / x_train) curve(sin(2*pi*x)/x, from = -pi, to = pi) points(x_train, y_train) # Only means: don't use Kxstarstar points(x_test, gpr_predict(Kxx, Kxstar, y_train), pch = 16) # Add credible intervals around predictions: gpr_fit <- gpr_predict(Kxx, Kxstar, y_train, Kxstarstar = Kxstarstar) segments(x0 = x_test,          y0 = gpr_fit$mean - 2 * sqrt(diag(gpr_fit$var)),          y1 = gpr_fit$mean + 2 * sqrt(diag(gpr_fit$var)), col = "#4DAF4A")

title: Sum of Kernels — kernel_sum
text: Builds kernel matrices list distances matrices computes sum.

title: Sum of Kernels — kernel_sum
code: kernel_sum(D2_list, length_scales, variances, kernels)

title: Sum of Kernels — kernel_sum
text: D2_list list matrices squared pairwise distances. length_scales vector length-scale parameters. variances vector kernel variance parameters. kernels vector kernels (rbf, matern05, matern15, matern25).

title: Sum of Kernels — kernel_sum
text: kernel matrix dimensions matrices D_list.

title: Sum of Kernels — kernel_sum
Matérn Kernels — matern
text: Builds Matérn kernel (smoothness parameter 0.5, 1.5 2.5) distance matrix.

title: Matérn Kernels — matern
code: matern(D, length_scale = 1, variance = 1, tau = 1.5, squared = TRUE)

title: Matérn Kernels — matern
text: D Matrix pairwise (possibly squared) distances. length_scale Length-scale parameter. variance Kernel variance parameter. tau Smoothness parameter. needs 0.5, 1.5 (default) 2.5. squared TRUE (default), D assumed contain squared distances. Set FALSE case.

title: Matérn Kernels — matern
text: kernel matrix dimensions D.

title: Matérn Kernels — matern
Sum of Matérn Kernels — matern_multiple
text: Builds Matérn kernel matrices list distances matrices computes sum.

title: Sum of Matérn Kernels — matern_multiple
code: matern_multiple(D_list, length_scales, variances, taus, squared = TRUE)

title: Sum of Matérn Kernels — matern_multiple
text: D_list list matrices pairwise (possibly squared) distances. length_scales vector length-scale parameters. variances vector kernel variance parameters. taus vector smoothness parameters. Values need 0.5, 1.5 2.5. single value provided, used components. squared TRUE (default), distance matrix assumed contain squared distances. FALSE, distance matrix assumed contain raw distances.

title: Sum of Matérn Kernels — matern_multiple
text: kernel matrix dimensions matrices D_list.

title: Sum of Matérn Kernels — matern_multiple

what: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
title: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
text: Computes average negative log predictive density (NLPD) test outcomes posterior Gaussian distribution returned GPR model. posterior covariance positive definite, small jitter term added main diagonal numerical stability; jitter term increased adaptively necessary.

title: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
code: nlpd_gpr(fit, y_test, add_noise = TRUE, nugget = 1e-10)

title: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
text: fit Gaussian process model fit obtained gpr_predict() fit_gpr(). y_test vector true test outcomes. add_noise TRUE (default), y_test assumed noisy observation noise added variance posterior covariance. nugget Numeric; base jitter added covariance diagonal needed ensure positive definiteness. Increased multiplicatively required.

title: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
text: scalar giving average NLPD. returned value attribute "jitter_used" indicating final diagonal jitter added (0 none).

title: Negative Log Predictive Density for Gaussian Process Regression Models — nlpd_gpr
code: set.seed(2026-06-25) N1 <- 50 N2 <- 20 x_train <- seq(-pi, pi, length.out = N1) x_test  <- runif(N2, -pi, pi) y_train <- x_train * plogis(x_train) * cos(x_train) + rnorm(N1, sd = 0.5) y_test <- x_test * plogis(x_test) * cos(x_test) D2 <- outer(c(x_train, x_test), c(x_train, x_test), "-")^2 fit <- fit_gpr(D2, seq_len(N1), N1 + seq_len(N2), y_train, y_test,                runs = 20L, cores = 1) #> Hyperparameter search 1 of 20. #> Optimum set at 65.1048849. #> Hyperparameter search 2 of 20. #> Current optimum improved from 65.1048849 to 57.4472943. #> Hyperparameter search 3 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 4 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 5 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 6 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 7 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 8 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 9 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 10 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 11 of 20. #> Current optimum improved from 57.4472943 to 40.4504072. #> Hyperparameter search 12 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 13 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 14 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 15 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 16 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 17 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 18 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 19 of 20. #> Hyperparameter search 20 of 20. # noisy outcome nlpd_gpr(fit, y_test + rnorm(N2, sd = 0.5)) #> [1] 1.130341 #> attr(,"jitter_used") #> [1] 0 # clean outcome nlpd_gpr(fit, y_test, add_noise = FALSE) #> Warning: Covariance matrix not positive definite. Trying jitter escalation. #> Cholesky succeeded with jitter = 1e-10. #> [1] -3.758439 #> attr(,"jitter_used") #> [1] 1e-10

what: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: Applies north-west corner algorithm obtain p-Wasserstein distance two one-dimensional empirical distributions.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
code: nw_corner_distance(   x,   y,   presorted = FALSE,   p = 2,   eps = sqrt(.Machine$double.eps) )

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: x, y Vectors representing one-dimensional empirical distributions. presorted Set TRUE x y sorted obtain speed-. p Order Wasserstein distance. Defaults p = 2. eps Numerical precision floating point comparisons.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
text: p-Wasserstein distance x y.

title: One-Dimensional Wasserstein Distance Using North-West Corner Algorithm — nw_corner_distance
code: x <- rnorm(10) y <- rnorm(40) nw_corner_distance(x, y) #> [1] 0.3434147 nw_corner_distance(sort(x), sort(y), presorted = TRUE) #> [1] 0.3434147

what: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf
title: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf
text: Builds Gaussian radial basis function kernel matrix distance matrix.

title: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf
code: rbf(D, length_scale = 1, variance = 1, squared = TRUE)

title: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf
text: D Matrix pairwise (possibly squared) distances. length_scale Length-scale parameter. variance Kernel variance parameter. squared TRUE (default), D assumed contain squared distances. Set FALSE case.

title: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf
text: kernel matrix dimensions D.

title: Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernel — rbf

what: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple
title: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple
text: Builds Gaussian radial basis function kernel matrices list distances matrices computes sum.

title: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple
code: rbf_multiple(D_list, length_scales, variances, squared = TRUE)

title: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple
text: D_list list matrices pairwise (possibly squared) distances. length_scales vector length-scale parameters. variances vector kernel variance parameters. squared TRUE (default), distance matrix assumed contain squared distances. FALSE, distance matrix assumed contain raw distances.

title: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple
text: kernel matrix dimensions matrices D_list.

title: Sum of Gaussian Radial Basis Function Kernels — rbf_multiple

what: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: Estimate sliced Wasserstein distance two empirical distributions represented matrices using Monte Carlo simulation. Optionally, matrix thetas can supplied. row thetas interpreted projection direction. rows unit vectors, allowing users transform projection directions rather matrices.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
code: sliced_wasserstein(x, y, p = 2, thetas = NULL, L = 50, seed = NULL)

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: x, y Matrices representing empirical distributions. p Order sliced Wasserstein distance. thetas Optionally, matrix, row represents projection direction. L thetas provided, L random projection directions generated. seed Optional random seed.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
text: Estimated sliced Wasserstein distance order p x y.

title: Sliced Wasserstein Distance Between Two Empirical Distributions — sliced_wasserstein
code: M1 <- matrix(rnorm(50), ncol = 5) M2 <- matrix(rnorm(250), ncol = 5) sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2) # random projection directions #> [1] 0.6542383 cardinal_axes <- diag(1, 5) sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2, thetas = cardinal_axes) #> [1] 0.6711259 first_two_axes <- cardinal_axes[1:2, ] sliced_wasserstein(M1, M2, thetas = first_two_axes) #> [1] 0.5846788
